author: @TimeSurfer206
text:
After pondering, I decided to go with this over Zinc Bromide. It just seems a lot safer. Read to the end for my favorite Bromine Story.
I would think that I would want to actually weigh and measure the amounts, if I were going to make an actual Battery of Cells, because I would want some consistency in things like, oh, Amp-Hour Capacity and such.
This is easy enough to do, and my Old Hippy Heart would be quite happy to be accurate to the tenth of a gram.


Here is my favorite Bromine story: When Thomas Midgley Junior was inventing Refrigerants, he found two he liked, and, rather arbitrarily and capriciously chose Chlorofluorocarbons over Bromofluorocarbons.
Which turned out to be a blessing, because, had he chosen Bromofluorocarbons, the Ozone Layer would have been destroyed before we even knew there was a problem.
I think so highly of the gentleman who invented TetraEthyl Lead, that I believe that if he had known about the Ozone Layer and the higher reactivity of the Bromoflorocarbons...
He would have gladly chosen them.

author: @maxkennedy5073
text: Hope you see this, crazy thought.  Zinc dendrites falling off or growing down to the halide layer increasing self discharge.  Most of the electrodes have been simple zinc plates bent horizontally and immersed near the top of the electrolyte.  Coat the bottom of the electrodes with a non conductive coating so the dendrites can't form on the bottom.  They grow up away from the halide layer and even breaking off they simply fall onto the electrode.  Seems too easy, what am I missing.  Lost surface area could be made up by bending electrode into an accordion shape.  Would the additional material be worth it?

author: @maxkennedy5073
text: I don't recall if you've ever done a calculation of energy capacity (wH or KwH) per mole of ZnI2.  Given there is the movement of 2 electrons per molecule, 6.022x10^23 molecules per mole that makes it 12.044x10^23 electrons moving per mole.  An amp is 6.24x10^18 electrons moving per second with 3600 seconds/hr so 6.24x10^18(3600) = 2.264x10^22 electrons per amp hour.  This gives (1.2044x10^24e/mole)/(2.264x10^22 e/aH) = 0. 532 x10^2 aH/m = 53.2 aH/m.  The literature seems to give cell voltages varying from 1.1v to 1.26v so for calculation purposes I'll us 1.2v.  1.2v x 53.2 aH/mole = 63.84 wH/m (watt hours per mole) theoretically.  There are always losses in the system so I'll assume a coulombic efficiency somewhere between lead acid at 85% and Lithium at 95% so 90% seems a reasonable 1st estimate.  therefore 63.84 x 0.9 = 57.456 wH/m.  This is all from 1st principles and there may be many factors I am not accounting for so I would ask is this at least in the right ballpark?  As it can achieve about a 14M solution does molarity have an effect on battery efficiency?

replies.author: @unicornadrian1358
replies.text:
How long is a piece of string? Variables include, but are not limited to, surface area of electrodes, distance between electrodes, concentration of solution.
Gather some materials and play with it. Build one with electrodes of a certain size and measure the output. Then increase the size by 100% and measure again. Try doubling the electrolyte and keeping the the electrodes the same size. Does the capacity double when you double the volume of electrolyte? Does capacity, current, or voltage change by changing the the electrode size? 
The beauty of getting down and dirty with a simple and cheap battery and playing around with it, is that you will gain a far greater understanding than watching the vids.

replies.author: @Spedley_2142
replies.text: @Unicorn Adrian I think you miss my point. There are many options to follow (i.e, currently I'm working on repurposing batteries from dicarded vapes) but i have the space for 5 tons of this stuff.  Which is more worth my while, give me a rough idea.

replies.author: @unicornadrian1358
replies.text: @Spedley_2142 again it depends what your goals are. I know nothing about vapes but assume they run lithium batteries. If you want more power density, then vape batteries win. If you want a battery that lasts for decades, can be sized up as large as you like, has zero risk of catastrophic failure, then Zinc Iodide is a better choice.

author: @wubwub89
text:
RM-S, I think I may have found a better use for some old hydranal t-5 I have (better than just disposing of it anyways). Might be a little more dangerous than sourcing the iodine in other ways, but I just have a couple liters of this stuff without much use for it anymore. I am in the USA, and as others have stated correctly, large volumes of iodine and some precursors are well regulated for their potential use in drug manufacturing and explosives (not very practical explosives for those curious, but nonetheless), and to be very clear in the comment I have zero intention of doing/enabling anyone to do any of this. Thanks for the idea... However, I digress. 

As a topic suggestion, it could be very useful for some viewers to learn/see some more about acid cells (I especially enjoyed the dilute pile cell group from a few months ago). I know this topic may require more caution, and it's not exactly a mystery to us all, but there is practical value in understanding how to create relatively high reserve  and low ESR(as a point of practical applications) cells.
Yes, you've definitely covered the principle well recently, and I do admit somewhat selfishly I'd like to see your personal take on a contained, independently sourced/designed/built conventional cell, more or less from a simulated circumstance of need (say, off the top of my head, post apocalyptic situations where you can't just go to the Auto store and purchase a large lead acid battery[ies]). Your chosen subjects in this field have been great to watch, and definitely informative, practical, beyond just tinkering, IMO. The zinc iodide videos are great examples of electrolytic storage and mechanisms, I'd just love to see it taken further with other chemistries, which I'm sure many will learn from at the very least. I have personally built a handful of various chemistry  acid cells, a select few with very good storage characteristics (albeit, some using a ludicrously expensive amount of purified laboratory grade acids and other materiels, but that's not necessary to demonstrate concepts obviously, I just use up extra/leftover/"expired" sources when I can to avoid waste. You have a talent for effective demonstration without breaking the bank, part of which I enjoy your channel so much for).

Thanks again for another great series of videos.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Galvanized fasteners and scraps of fence wire would work as well.

The only reason I even mention that is because I have just a whole lot of them left over from the results of the former land owner, as well as generous donations of used fence wire from people in my community, out of which, some is only good for scrap, but I carry it home anyhow.

If you want another source for zinc, old carburetors, old car door handles, and some old gas plumbing valves are also made of zinc [its much heavier than aluminum, and it will burn with a toxic powdery smoke if you scrape some chips off and ignite them with a torch], and zinc also melts almost as cool as lead [you can melt zinc in an aluminum pan, but they might amalgamate, so I would not advise doing so, unless you never intend to cook with the pan after that].

So far as making big hunks of zinc into powder, simply put a belt sander upside down in a box, or suspend it over a plastic bag so you catch all the dust inside of it... Or you could set it up with a vacuum cleaner to suck up all the dust as you produce it, but either way, of course, be sure not to breathe it as it can harm or even kill you.

That can get you some zinc pretty quickly, and not only can you make powder out of it, since it melts at such a low temperature, you can make bars out of it to use as your electrodes as well.

Of course, molten zinc can ignite in the air, so be sure you do not overheat it.

If you use a steel mold for your bars, be sure it is NOT 'clean' because it is possible for the molten zinc to directly bond its self to the clean steel.

I would suggest that you ever so slightly oxidize the surface of a steel mold, should you go with steel, so that it will have a surface that the zinc will not want to bond to, and make sure that the corners are radiused, and that you do not make the mold any deeper than the thickness of the bars/plates you wish to make, because otherwise it can be a real buggar to get them out.

While zinc will naturally want to form a sheet about 3 milimeters thick you can press form it much thinner, if you are looking for thin sheets.

My approach to that would be to get some flat sheets of steel, stack them together with some washers in the corners that space the plates the thickness you want the zinc to be, then place them into a removable container.

Since making a removable container might be hard if you use steel, you might want to make it out of fire brick with the edges sealed with regular plaster.

This will allow you to separate it with little more than a light tap.

In fact, since it might come apart even more easily than that, it would be a good idea to put little notches at the corners, and use some wire to hold it together, before you apply the plaster, so as to ensure it remains in one piece long enough to get your pour done.

If the steel plates fit into the brick housing snugly enough, they will not wiggle about, but even if they do, it should not make much of a mess.

You would want to pre-heat the mold before pouring, so as to ensure no bubbles form, messing up your plates.

Once it cools, you can simply unscrew the stacks of plates, and so long as the zinc does not bond to the steel, you should have no problem pulling them apart.

If you use zinc plated washers, there is a potential benefit to this, in that it might be possible to use them as pre-made eyelets to hold the finished zinc plates by.

If the zinc washers are firmly bonded to the zinc plate, they will save one time if they want to have holes in the plates from which to suspend the plates in the iodide solution.

Otherwise, one should use stainless or black steel washers to prevent them from sticking.

Also you can electroplate zinc, and I would suggest using a piece of copper foil because you can easily shape it, it will carry a decent level of current density and help you obtain a reasonably uniform plating product.

And when you are done, you can just peel the copper tape off of it.

text: Iodine is freely available in vetinary clinics used to keep wounds sterile

replies.author: @chemistryofquestionablequa6252
replies.text: That's povidone iodine which has a polymer that makes it REALLY awkward to get the iodine out. Much better to buy an iodide salt off Amazon or ebay,  convert it to iodine,  then zinc iodide.

replies.author: @SasquatchTrevor
replies.text: Nilered did a video some time ago about extracting iodine from povidone iodine.  He was able to extract 2 g/500 ml.  He stated that it was too much of a hassle and too expensive to get iodine that way and that it was much cheaper to just buy it.

author: @SasquatchTrevor
text: A word of caution for anyone in the States trying to get ahold of iodine.  Iodine is heavily regulated due to its use in making methamphetamine.  In the States, you buy can buy up to 200 g/yr  (100 g/yr if you are living in Cali) with no problem, but after that, you need to obtain a license for anything above 200 g.  Just wanted to let everyone know about this rule.  It's absurd, but it's how we do things in the States.

replies.author: @chemistryofquestionablequa6252
replies.text: You can buy iodide by the pound and convert it to iodine,  it's incredibly easy and there are preps all over YouTube

replies.author: @ProlificInvention
replies.text: It seems they'd regulate it also as it's used in Ammonium Tri Iodide crystals: A contact explosive

replies.author: @chemistryofquestionablequa6252
replies.text: @ProlificInvention that stuff really is just a novelty.  It's far too sensitive to have any practical applications as an explosive.
